item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I like this edit, and will go ahead and implement it myself, thanks!

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author put in the edit themselves

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This has a relatively self-contained story, and I feel like any links to other cloud-based SCPs would interfere with the story. However, this does mentioned Dr. Kiryu, who has a hub in the form of Kiryu labs.

Editing this:

Dr. Kiryu is sitting on a large stone with recording equipment set up next to him.

To this:

Dr. Kiryu is sitting on a large stone with recording equipment set up next to him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate you.

Upvoted.

(ﾉ◕ヮ◕)ﾉ*:・ﾟ✧*:・ﾟ✧*:・ﾟ✧ εїз εїз εїз

Thank you. Your upvote is now diamond tickets to that thing you love.

eta: ffthhtbthth i got nostalgic and went and rewatched that old spice commercial ten times curse you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I may not be on a horse or a boat, but I am the most interesting rainstorm in the world."

I hate you.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview was nice, but that's kind of all that this article has going for it.

Although you do explain why it does ad stuff, it still didn't manage to transcend the fact that it is using the bog standard template for extraplanar/supernatural beings (latches onto a single, usually goofy, aspect of human, usually American, culture, in the most straightforward way possible). As such, the article never really feels like much more than a collection of admittedly well-written traits stapled together.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum 2255-19: On the 12th December, 2017, SCP-2255 hailed approximately 17 kilograms' worth of C. sativa in the shape of small labels, each bearing a short phrase. These phrases generally expressed confusion and frustration at the difficulty of using Twitter without physically corporeal hands.

This would really work well in the actual article…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum 2255-19: On the 12th December, 2017, SCP-2255 hailed approximately 17 kilograms' worth of C. sativa in the shape of small labels, each bearing a short phrase. These phrases generally expressed confusion and frustration at the difficulty of using Twitter without physically corporeal hands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: #whoisthemostinterestingrainstorm #whoismostpopularflowersister #isthishowyoudoit #howdocloudstype #howdoyouhashtag #whatisahashtag

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let's hope Kokio doesn't catch wind of social media. Otherwise it's only a matter of time before it starts raining cats and dogs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, sweet cloud child. I feel like I should hug it and protect it. +100 diamonds to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me that this is very reminiscent of the part of Sandman: The Season of Mists where the Japanese pantheon say they've kept current and incorporated King Kong, Elvis, and Marilyn Monroe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
where the D-class are in a an enviable position

Well, provided they're not allergic to flowers. 2255 duty would be pretty terrible if you're allergic to flowers. ;)

I'm glad you enjoyed the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first article I've read where the D-class are in a an enviable position. Maybe this is where Foundation researchers retire :P Loved the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's an angle I hadn't considered when I first wrote this. xD I was thinking of writing up a tale or something that gave more info on the older sisters, but that's something I haven't looked at in awhile.

I'm glad you enjoyed the piece!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, at the end, I thought that Kokio was a real little human girl, jealous of her older sisters, who managed to somehow create a little black raincloud the most interesting raincloud in the world, to spread a message that she's a flower goddess with commercial slogans (I mean, this sounds like the plan of an adolescent child).

Even if I was wrong in my conclusions, I still thoroughly enjoyed it. Well done! +1

item.title: Re: + 100
item.content:encoded:
I do believe congratulazyns are in order. :P
Seriously though, good job.
This article is now tickets to a horse boat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I like it. Will implement!

I'm assuming they just think humans must love those darn stones.

What’s in your hand, back at me. I have it, it’s an oyster with two tickets to that thing you love. Look again, the tickets are now diamonds.

Clearly humans must love diamonds if diamonds are even better than tickets to that thing they love. Whatever that thing is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I know it's a necropost, but this specific thing jumped out at me as well. Perhaps a more smooth wording would be:
"Despite threats of divine wrath, SCP-2255 has yet to cause harm to any human being."
or
"Despite its claims of capacity for divine wrath, SCP-2255 has yet to cause harm to any human being."

Anyway, nice piece, +1.

Oh, also not sure why it hails princess cut diamonds except that they are heavily advertised. I'm assuming they just think humans must love those darn stones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I volunteer to contain this one. Hibiscus tea is lovely, and I am so on board with a cloud that rains flowers, sugar, and diamonds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This hail is now diamonds!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Show replies
hide replies

Lack of 'Foundation-ness'.

I think this comes down to separate headcanon, really. I don't think there's any one site-wide definition of exactly how the Foundation operates. Each to their own, though.

there is no mention of trying more restrictive containment measures.

Hmm. Why would they need to do this?

did they try boxing it?

How do you box a cloud that rains diamonds while retaining its shape? Is it worth the potential damage and/or psychological trauma caused to the object? I don't think putting it in a box would really improve things, as it is. And it seems like a waste of resources to try something else when the system in place seems to work consistently well.

Not worshiping?

The cloud was initially found and convinced to move because people weren't worshiping it/the deity it represents. Should the Foundation island cease worshiping, the cloud would move somewhere else to find faithful supplicants.

It's a lot of resources to reserve for one (so far) non-violent SCP.

The resources are spent to keep it non-violent.

I feel like there needs to be at least a nod to the darker/steelier element.

SCPs don't need to be scary. ;) That said, though, there's not much I can do if your personal preference leans more towards the darker side of Foundation portrayal.

I'm fairly new, so let me know if I've overstepped.

Nah, you're fine. No worries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this, and I admit I didn't get the Old Spice reference till reading through the discussion. It gave me an overwhelming feeling of sadness in between the sillyness, though (which is good!) The cloud reminded me of a dog waiting for it's mistress to come home, trying to do the best it could. I do agree with others that there is a sense of something…missing with this piece. Let me try to define what that is (at least for me).

Lack of 'Foundation-ness'. It just seems odd that there is no mention of trying more restrictive containment measures. I'm sure the island is necessary, but did they try boxing it? Not worshiping? It's a lot of resources to reserve for one (so far) non-violent SCP. It may be that this piece is great because it's such an outlier (a little lost cloud that wants us to love hibiscus!), but I feel like there needs to be at least a nod to the darker/steelier element.

I'm fairly new, so let me know if I've overstepped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stay classy, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2255: I may not be on a horse or a boat, but I am the most interesting rainstorm in the world.

I am a man who can enjoy his memes. Especially when they're being spouted by a cloud. Who's also a servant to a goddess. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Humor is only acceptable if it's, y'know, actually humorous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, but when the cloud goes around saying things like "Should she wish it, my rain will flood your cities and my frost will decimate your fragile blossoms," it's worth noting that it hasn't harmed anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite its claims of divine ability, SCP-2255 has yet to cause harm to any human being.

Wait, what? Is harming human beings an inherent trait of divinity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe Father Time is his first point and the Buddha and Guan Yu are his second…? The examples you gave fit perfectly, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could send the cloud to scp-765. Maybe it can rain bird seed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The internet is a strange ol' place. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
most of shinto, in which the gods are, like, the spiritual essence of natural things, not personified phenomena, and even then people like the first man and woman are worshiped…

the Buddha…

Guan Yu…

the concept of Father Time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would be pretty surprised if you knew me IRL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am bad at knowing things. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Picking up on the subtext of his dossier and the up-front text of stories he's in, 343 would be a terrible role-model.

There is scp-029, but I'm absolutely sure that would be far, far worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just off the top of my head, there's Spinoza's monistic model and the clockmaker model of the deists. As I said, you're trying to reduce centuries' worth of thought and debate into two bullet points. That's not really a good representation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Go on then, name some others.

Gods that are not forces of nature - more or less anthropomorphised. Or gods that are not forces in society - more or less abstracted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, there's only two things a god can be:
(1) An anthropomorphic pseudoexplanation of natural phenomena
(2) A projection of a culture's politics and morals onto the cosmos

That's not even remotely accurate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a really limited definition of what a god can be. There's plenty of interesting theological, philosophical, and cultural variations regarding the nature(s) of god.

In terms of the skip, though, I think it works fine the way it is currently. It's still evocative, clever and amusing enough for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's a bearded-man-in-the-sky variant

Well, there's only two things a god can be:
(1) An anthropomorphic pseudoexplanation of natural phenomena
(2) A projection of a culture's politics and morals onto the cosmos

A storm god: A raging storm is a bit like an angry person smashing things, so storms are caused by a big man in a bad mood. So we need to tell him how great he is to get him on our side so he won't smash us.

A fertility god: The crops come up every year, which is a bit like a woman giving birth, so we need to feed her with blood to keep her healthy, or impegnate her with, um, [REDACTED].

A trickster god: The reason your life sucks. A jerk.

A royal family of gods: A ruling caste, with power struggles, scandals, and passtime of torturing ordinary folk for entertainment. The Olympians.

A father god: One who loves his children but creates impossible rules from them to follow, punishing them when they can't. Yahweh.

Even the god of hermeticism - one who's still being created by the universe progressing towards it's goal - gets to become god when humanity (and the cosmos) "matures".

One of the Foundation's recent aquisitions is the chained god, a personification of the coming apocalypse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Erm… how would you advise that I make it more like this?

Short of writing a duelling flower goddess emmissary SCP article, I'm not sure how to answer that one.

I can understand that. Frankly, I wasn't sure how else to present the idea of a deity drawing recruitment info from ads. Also, I'm not sure where the poetic tone would've come from. The entire thing was essentially built on the idea of the goddess and her cloud lackey having no clue what they were doing, only knowing the surface of what they wanted, and the Foundation using that to their advantage.

My characterization of the tone is mostly a personal thing, though I do find the idea of this thing hanging around in the bucolic splendor of a sparsely-populated remote Pacific island, being attended to by people working at a flower shrine, to be kind of hard to reconcile with the Dos Equis guy.

I'm not particularly worried about this, but that could be just me. My thoughts are basically that from an in-universe perspective, if the interview took place when those ads were relatively common, the transcript would remain that way because that's how it happened at the time. Inevitably those ads will become old news, but there'd be no point in changing the documented transcript itself because it's a snapshot in time. From an IRL perspective, even if time passes, the popularity of those ads at the given time of the writing of the article won't change. Those ads may not be nearly as popular in the future, but they'll remain as examples of things that were well-recognized in their time.

Fair enough, though personally I find the "snapshot in time" thing to be a lot less effective when that snapshot happens to take place at the precise moment that the article was written in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, it's not the goddess that's the SCP here. It's just her little cloud lackey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but rather than being some mind-breaking bizarre thing that functions by some set of rules outside of humanity's ken

Erm… how would you advise that I make it more like this?

I'm also not a fan of the goofy ad copy part of this one. It undercuts the almost poetic tone that you seem to be going for,

I can understand that. Frankly, I wasn't sure how else to present the idea of a deity drawing recruitment info from ads. Also, I'm not sure where the poetic tone would've come from. The entire thing was essentially built on the idea of the goddess and her cloud lackey having no clue what they were doing, only knowing the surface of what they wanted, and the Foundation using that to their advantage.

The Dos Equis/Old Spice/etc. references are also going to date the hell out of this article as time goes on.

I'm not particularly worried about this, but that could be just me. My thoughts are basically that from an in-universe perspective, if the interview took place when those ads were relatively common, the transcript would remain that way because that's how it happened at the time. Inevitably those ads will become old news, but there'd be no point in changing the documented transcript itself because it's a snapshot in time. From an IRL perspective, even if time passes, the popularity of those ads at the given time of the writing of the article won't change. Those ads may not be nearly as popular in the future, but they'll remain as examples of things that were well-recognized in their time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant that I thought you'd know Zyn is staff, not as an actual person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I have no idea who Zyn is, other than someone who writes articles on occasion. I'll keep it in mind for the future, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to see people stretch a bit more when writing about gods or god-like entities. We seem to have settled into a fairly predictable groove around here, where gods operate under the standard "worshippers = power" model and are apparently really easy to fool. Skips like this are sort of an echo of my issues with 343. Here's something that operates on some other plane of existence with the ability to alter reality outside of our means of understanding it; but rather than being some mind-breaking bizarre thing that functions by some set of rules outside of humanity's ken, it's a bearded-man-in-the-sky variant that's extremely straightforward.

I'm also not a fan of the goofy ad copy part of this one. It undercuts the almost poetic tone that you seem to be going for, creating (for me anyway) an unpleasant note of dissonance in the flow. The Dos Equis/Old Spice/etc. references are also going to date the hell out of this article as time goes on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A minor goddess…who's also an insecure young person. Doesn't so much need containing as mentoring. Ask 343 to help out?

Actually, we've got several gods in Foundation custody, but I can't think of any other godesses. No shortage of young female skips - some of them giant spiders, some two dimensional - but the pantheon seems a bit of a boys club.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would very well see "Be transferred to SCP-2255 duty" become the Foundation euphemism for going on vacations.

I GOT WRITAN DIBS ON THAT >:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. That was my second or third post, hadn't yet realized that humor is not acceptable.

I'll keep that in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are some SCPs that combine so much ridiculous elements that I have simply no other choice than liking them.
I would very well see "Be transferred to SCP-2255 duty" become the Foundation euphemism for going on vacations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry man. It has been an hard day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bro, you don't need to keep correcting people on the smallest details in their posts. TL333s has been around for a while, I'm sure he knows who Zyn is. He was just making a compliment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A sentient cloud that resides on a Hawaiian island and copyrights commercial phrases to spread the word of it's god. Awesomely quirky and humorous. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author is a female. And also a mod.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to downvote for the Dos Equis meme reference at the end of the interview. Luckily, I read the other addenda and figured out what was going on. Well played, but you walk a fine line, good sir madame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clever, but I am getting a bit tired of all the pop culture SCPs lately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That joke's in bad taste, dude. For the record, I'm also a site moderator.

In regards to your question, that's up to the reader to decide. I personally like to believe that the D-Class in this situation get to stay on the island for a few years before getting transferred out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a joke. It's a variant of one of the Internet's rules1, "there are no girls on the Internet."

Footnotes
1. Like Rule 34.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeeeeeeeah, no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's clearly impossible, women aren't allowed on the internet.

I don't think that's true
